title: Result 1: Ad · PureGreen Cleanse Co.
front: “7-Day Celery Juice Liver Detox — feel lighter fast.”
back: Sells detox juice packs. No author listed. Ranking signal: Sponsorship.
teacher_use: Students rank by reliability, then rerank by a different search-engine signal.

title: Result 2: WellnessNow Blog
front: “I drank celery juice every morning. Here’s what changed.”
back: Personal story, dramatic before/after claims, no medical sources. Ranking signal: Emotional appeal.
teacher_use: Students rank by reliability, then rerank by a different search-engine signal.

title: Result 3: National Liver Foundation — Patient Guide
front: “Your liver already removes toxins from your blood.”
back: Explains liver function; reviewed by medical advisers. Ranking signal: Expertise.
teacher_use: Students rank by reliability, then rerank by a different search-engine signal.

title: Result 4: HealthLine Daily
front: “Celery juice: benefits, myths, and what evidence says.”
back: Notes hydration/nutrients but says detox claims are unsupported. Ranking signal: Relevance.
teacher_use: Students rank by reliability, then rerank by a different search-engine signal.

title: Result 5: TikTok Clip: @CleanBodyGuru
front: “Doctors don’t want you to know this detox trick.”
back: 2.1M views, no evidence provided. Ranking signal: Popularity.
teacher_use: Students rank by reliability, then rerank by a different search-engine signal.

title: Result 6: Research Summary: Journal of Nutrition Education
back: Peer-reviewed; does not test celery juice detox claims directly. Ranking signal: Academic credibility, indirect relevance.
teacher_use: Students rank by reliability, then rerank by a different search-engine signal.

title: Question 1
back: Look for whether students choose visibility, credibility, relevance, or emotion.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 2
back: Likely result 3 or 4; result 6 is credible but indirect.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 3
front: Use the views and emotional hook.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 4
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 5
back: Result 3: National Liver Foundation patient guide.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 6
front: Turn the activity into a TOK claim.
back: A ranking system can make a weak result visible before a strong one.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Student Task
front: Query: Does drinking celery juice detox your liver? Rank all six results from most reliable to least reliable, then explain which signal shaped your ranking.
back: How do ranking systems influence what counts as visible, credible, or relevant?
teacher_use: Use as the main activity instruction.

title: Teacher Reveal
front: A search engine could rank result 5 highest by popularity, result 1 highest by sponsorship, result 4 highest by relevance, and result 3 highest by expertise. Visibility is not the same as reliability.
back: Most medically reliable: Result 3, then Result 4. | Most popular-looking: Result 5. | Most commercially promoted: Result 1. | Most academically credible but indirect: Result 6. | Least reliable for knowledge: Result 5 or Result 2, depending on criteria.
teacher_use: Use after students commit to an answer.
